1:
"Paul and Timotheus, the servants of Jesus Christ, to all the saints in Christ Jesus which are at Philippi, with the bishops and deacons:" The witnesses note the greeting to the saints, bishops, and deacons — with the question of bishop and presbyter. On "Paul and Timotheus, the servants of Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the humility. Barnes and Ellicott note that Paul, writing to a beloved and trusting church, does not assert his apostleship but joins Timothy with himself simply as "servants (bondservants) of Jesus Christ" — a title of affectionate humility.

On "with the bishops and deacons," interpreters address the offices. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley, hold that "bishop (episkopos, overseer)" and "presbyter (elder)" name the same office in the New Testament — the two are used interchangeably (Acts 20:17, 28; Titus 1:5-7), so the "bishops" here are simply the plural body of pastors of the one church, not prelates of a superior order. Barnes argues at length against the prelatical reading, noting there were but two orders (bishops/elders and deacons) and a plurality of "bishops" in one city. Ellicott grants this identity yet holds that the later episcopate (one bishop over the presbyters) developed providentially in the early second century, under the sanction of St. John.

The witnesses note the two offices: bishops (elders) and deacons, the latter charged with the alms and care of the poor. The verse displays the greeting to the saints, bishops, and deacons.

The witnesses agree the substance: Paul and Timothy write as "servants of Christ" to "all the saints at Philippi, with the bishops and deacons" — and that "bishop" and "presbyter (elder)" name the same New Testament office, used interchangeably (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley); the two church offices being bishops/elders and deacons, the latter for the alms and poor. But the bearing divides them: Barnes argues forcefully against the prelatical view of three distinct orders (a plurality of bishops in one city refuting it); Ellicott, granting the NT identity, holds the monarchical episcopate a later, legitimate development under St. John; Calvin calls the monepiscopate a human custom of later origin. The verse displays the greeting and the polity crux. The verse marks the servants: Paul and Timothy. The verse displays the saints: at Philippi. The verse displays the debate: bishop and presbyter. The verse displays the offices: bishops and deacons. The greeting displays the two NT offices and the identity of bishop and elder. The verse displays the humility: servants. The verse displays the bishops: the elders. The verse displays the deacons: for the poor. The greeting to the saints, bishops, and deacons displays the opening of the warmest of Paul's letters and a polity crux: Paul and Timothy greet the church with its two orders of officers — bishops (the elders/overseers, the same office under either name, against the prelatical three-order reading) and deacons. The verse displays the apostolic greeting, completed by grace and peace (v.2).
2:
"Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the apostolic blessing of grace and peace. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Gill note Paul's customary greeting, nearly the same in all his epistles: "grace (free favour) be unto you, and peace (its fruit)" — the twofold blessing, grace the source and peace the effect.

On "from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Henry note the joint source: grace and peace come "from God our Father" (the fountain of all blessing) and "from the Lord Jesus Christ" (the channel through whom they reach us) — Father and Son named together.

The puritan Henry notes the order: there is no peace without grace, for inward peace springs from a sense of God's favour; and no grace and peace but from the Father through Christ the Mediator. The verse displays the apostolic blessing of grace and peace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's customary greeting — "grace be unto you, and peace," grace the source and peace its fruit (Barnes, Gill); coming "from God our Father (the fountain) and from the Lord Jesus Christ (the channel)," Father and Son together (Barnes, Henry). Henry notes no peace without grace, no grace but from the Father through Christ. The verse displays grace and peace. The verse marks the blessing: grace and peace. The verse displays the source: Father and Son. The verse displays the order: grace then peace. The verse displays the channel: Christ the Mediator. The blessing displays grace and peace from Father and Son. The verse displays the grace: free favour. The verse displays the peace: its fruit. The verse displays the source: God and Christ. The apostolic blessing of grace and peace displays the opening prayer of the epistle: Paul invokes upon the Philippians grace — God's free favour — and peace, its fruit, both flowing from God the Father, through the Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the greeting, opening into thanksgiving (v.3).
3:
"I thank my God upon every remembrance of you," The brief verse notes Paul's thanksgiving at every remembrance of them. The grammatical voices note the gratitude. Barnes and Robertson note Paul opens his thanksgiving: "I thank my God upon every remembrance (at all my recollection) of you" — every time he thinks of the Philippians, his heart turns to grateful thanks to God.

On "my God," the witnesses note the relation. Barnes and Calvin note the affectionate "my God" — Paul claims God as his own in covenant relation; his thanks flow from a personal, intimate faith.

The puritan Henry notes the warmth: the Philippians, who had been so good to Paul, are remembered with unmixed joy; he thanks God for the grace and usefulness he sees in them. The verse displays Paul's thanksgiving at every remembrance of them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opens his thanksgiving — "I thank my God upon every remembrance of you," every recollection of the Philippians turning to grateful thanks (Barnes, Robertson); the affectionate "my God," claiming God as his own in covenant relation (Barnes, Calvin); the Philippians remembered with unmixed joy, Paul thanking God for the grace in them (Henry). The verse displays his thanksgiving. The verse marks the thanks: to my God. The verse displays the occasion: every remembrance. The verse displays the relation: my God. The verse displays the warmth: unmixed joy. The thanksgiving displays Paul grateful for the Philippians. The verse displays the thanks: continual. The verse displays the remembrance: of them. The verse displays the God: his own. Paul's thanksgiving at every remembrance of them displays the warmth of his affection: every time the Philippians come to his mind, his heart overflows in grateful thanks to God — his own God — for the grace he sees in them. The verse displays the apostle's gratitude, joined to joyful prayer (v.4). What is striking in this opening is the warmth of it, for the apostle does not begin, as he must sometimes do, with reproof or anxious correction, but with a thanksgiving so full and unmixed that it sets the tone of the whole letter; the Philippians were a church whose faith and love had given him cause not for grief but for gratitude, and the very memory of them moved him to bless the God who had wrought such grace in them.
4:
"Always in every prayer of mine for you all making request with joy," The brief verse notes Paul's joyful prayer for them all. The grammatical voices note the constancy. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul prays "always in every prayer of mine for you all" — his intercession for the Philippians is constant, embracing the whole church ("you all," a phrase frequent in this epistle).

On "making request with joy," the witnesses note the gladness. Barnes and Calvin note the distinctive note of this epistle: Paul makes his "request with joy" — his prayers for them are not anxious or grieved (as for some churches) but full of gladness, for they gave him so much cause for rejoicing.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the affection: the joy that marks Paul's prayers for the Philippians reflects the peculiar love and confidence between him and this church. The verse displays Paul's joyful prayer for them all.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul prays "always in every prayer of mine for you all," his intercession constant and embracing the whole church (Barnes, Robertson); "making request with joy," his prayers full of gladness not grief, for they gave him cause for rejoicing (Barnes, Calvin); the joy reflecting the peculiar love between him and the church (Wesley). The verse displays joyful prayer. The verse marks the constancy: always, every prayer. The verse displays the scope: for you all. The verse displays the joy: with gladness. The verse displays the affection: peculiar love. The prayer displays Paul interceding for them with joy. The verse displays the praying: always. The verse displays the request: for all. The verse displays the joy: in it. Paul's joyful prayer for them all displays the gladness of his love: he prays constantly for the whole church, and makes his requests with joy — for the Philippians, unlike some, gave him cause not for grief but for rejoicing in his every prayer. The verse displays the apostle's joyful intercession, grounded in their fellowship in the gospel (v.5). The note of joy that here breaks in is the keynote of the whole epistle, sounded again and again through every chapter; even from a prison, and under the shadow of a possible death, the apostle prays for this beloved church not with the heaviness of fear but with a gladness that springs from his confidence in God and his deep affection for them, so that intercession itself becomes for him an occasion of rejoicing.
5:
"For your fellowship in the gospel from the first day until now;" The brief verse notes their fellowship in the gospel from the first day. The grammatical voices note the ground of joy. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for Paul's joyful thanks: "your fellowship (koinonia — partnership, sharing, participation) in the gospel" — the Philippians' active partnership in the cause of the gospel, including their support of Paul.

On "from the first day until now," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Calvin note their perseverance: this fellowship had continued "from the first day (of their conversion) until now" — steadfast and unbroken; they had not, like some, grown cold but persevered in gospel partnership.

The grammatical-historical note the breadth: "fellowship in the gospel" includes both their sharing in its blessings and their active participation in its furtherance (by their gifts, prayers, and steadfastness). The verse displays their fellowship in the gospel from the first day.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of Paul's joy — "your fellowship (partnership, sharing) in the gospel," the Philippians' active partnership in the gospel cause, including their support (Barnes, Robertson); "from the first day until now," steadfast and unbroken perseverance from their conversion (Barnes, Calvin); the fellowship including both sharing its blessings and furthering it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays gospel fellowship. The verse marks the fellowship: in the gospel. The verse displays the constancy: first day until now. The verse displays the partnership: active. The verse displays the perseverance: unbroken. The fellowship displays the Philippians' steadfast gospel partnership. The verse displays the fellowship: shared. The verse displays the gospel: the cause. The verse displays the perseverance: from the first. Their fellowship in the gospel from the first day displays the reason for Paul's joyful thanks: the Philippians had been steadfast partners in the gospel cause — sharing its blessings and furthering its work — from the day of their conversion until now, persevering where others grew cold. The verse displays their gospel partnership, the ground of Paul's confidence (v.6). The word he chooses, fellowship or partnership, is rich with meaning, for it embraces not only their gifts sent to support him in his need but their whole active share in the work and furtherance of the gospel; they had thrown in their lot with him and with the cause of Christ from the very first day, and had held to that partnership without wavering, so that their steadfast generosity and constancy were themselves the ground of the grateful joy with which the apostle remembered them.
6:
"Being confident of this very thing, that he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note Paul's confidence that God will complete the good work he began. On "he which hath begun a good work in you," the grammatical voices note the divine author. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's assured confidence: "he (God) which hath begun a good work in you" — the work of grace in the Philippians is God's own work, begun by him in their conversion and regeneration.

On "will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the completion. Barnes and Calvin note the promise: God "will perform (carry on, complete, perfect) it until the day of Jesus Christ" — God will not abandon what he began but will carry the work of grace through to completion at Christ's return; the perseverance of the saints rests on God's faithfulness.

The Reformed Calvin notes the ground: the believer's confidence is not in himself but in God, who, having begun the work freely, will faithfully finish it; what God begins he completes. The puritan Henry adds that we must not trust outward appearances but only the new creation God works. The verse displays Paul's confidence that God will complete the good work.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "he (God) which hath begun a good work in you," the work of grace God's own, begun in conversion (Barnes, Robertson); God "will perform (complete, perfect) it until the day of Jesus Christ," carrying it through to completion at Christ's return, the perseverance of the saints resting on God's faithfulness (Barnes, Calvin); the confidence in God who finishes what he begins (Calvin, Henry). The verse displays God completing his work. The verse marks the author: God began it. The verse displays the work: of grace. The verse displays the completion: the day of Christ. The verse displays the ground: God's faithfulness. The confidence displays God carrying his work to completion. The verse displays the beginning: by God. The verse displays the performing: by God. The verse displays the day: of Christ. Paul's confidence that God will complete the good work he began displays the ground of the believer's assurance: the work of grace is God's own, begun by him and certain to be carried through to completion at the day of Jesus Christ — for what God begins, his faithfulness ensures he will finish. The verse displays the perseverance of the saints, grounded in Paul's love for them (v.7).
7:
"Even as it is meet for me to think this of you all, because I have you in my heart; inasmuch as both in my bonds, and in the defence and confirmation of the gospel, ye all are partakers of my grace." The witnesses note Paul's love for the Philippians as partakers of his grace. On "it is meet for me to think this of you all, because I have you in my heart," the grammatical voices note the affection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's ground for his confidence: it is "meet (right, fitting)" for him to think well of them, "because I have you in my heart" — he carries the Philippians in his deepest affection.

On "both in my bonds, and in the defence and confirmation of the gospel," the witnesses note their partnership. Barnes and Calvin note that the Philippians had shared with Paul "both in my bonds (his imprisonment) and in the defence and confirmation (the vindicating and establishing) of the gospel" — they had stood with him in his sufferings and his gospel work.

On "ye all are partakers of my grace," the puritan Henry notes the fellowship: they are "partakers (joint-sharers) of my grace" — sharing in the grace given to Paul, whether the grace of suffering for Christ or the grace of the gospel ministry; their fellow-suffering binds them to him. The verse displays Paul's love for the Philippians as partakers of his grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: it is "meet (fitting)" for Paul to think well of them, "because I have you in my heart," carrying them in deepest affection (Barnes, Robertson); they had shared "both in my bonds and in the defence and confirmation of the gospel," standing with him in sufferings and gospel work (Barnes, Calvin); "partakers of my grace," joint-sharers in the grace given to Paul, their fellow-suffering binding them (Henry). The verse displays partakers of grace. The verse marks the affection: you in my heart. The verse displays the partnership: in bonds and gospel. The verse displays the sharing: of grace. The verse displays the bond: fellow-suffering. The love displays the Philippians sharing Paul's grace and sufferings. The verse displays the heart: holding them. The verse displays the bonds: shared. The verse displays the grace: partaken. Paul's love for the Philippians as partakers of his grace displays the depth of their bond: he carries them in his heart, for they had shared with him in his bonds and in the defence of the gospel — joint-partakers of the grace given him, fellow-sufferers in the cause of Christ. The verse displays the apostle's affection, deepened to a yearning love (v.8).
8:
"For God is my record, how greatly I long after you all in the bowels of Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes Paul's yearning love for them in the affection of Christ. The grammatical voices note the solemn appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul calls God to witness the depth of his love: "God is my record (witness), how greatly I long after (yearn for, deeply desire) you all" — he appeals to God himself for the sincerity and intensity of his affection.

On "in the bowels of Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the depth. Barnes and Chrysostom note the strong figure: "in the bowels (splanchna — the inward parts, the seat of tender affection) of Jesus Christ" — Paul loves them not merely with his own love but with the very tenderness of Christ, as though Christ's own heart of compassion beat in him.

The puritan Henry notes the union: Paul's affection for the Philippians flows from his union with Christ; he loves them with Christ's love, in Christ's heart. The verse displays Paul's yearning love for them in the affection of Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul calls God to witness — "God is my record, how greatly I long after (yearn for) you all," appealing to God for the sincerity of his love (Barnes, Robertson); "in the bowels (the tender inward affection) of Jesus Christ," loving them with the very tenderness of Christ (Barnes, Chrysostom); his affection flowing from union with Christ (Henry). The verse displays yearning love. The verse marks the witness: God is my record. The verse displays the longing: greatly I long. The verse displays the depth: the bowels of Christ. The verse displays the union: Christ's love. The love displays Paul yearning with Christ's own affection. The verse displays the record: God. The verse displays the longing: intense. The verse displays the affection: of Christ. Paul's yearning love for them in the affection of Christ displays the depth of his heart: calling God to witness, he declares how greatly he longs for them all — loving them not with mere human affection but with the very tenderness and compassion of Jesus Christ. The verse displays the apostle's Christ-filled love, expressed in prayer for their growth (vv.9-11).
9:
"And this I pray, that your love may abound yet more and more in knowledge and in all judgment;" The brief verse notes Paul's prayer that their love abound in knowledge and discernment. The grammatical voices note the petition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's prayer for the Philippians: "that your love may abound (overflow, increase) yet more and more" — not that they begin to love, for they already do, but that their love grow ever greater.

On "in knowledge and in all judgment," the witnesses note the guidance of love. Barnes and Calvin note that the love is to abound "in knowledge (epignosis — full, accurate knowledge) and in all judgment (aisthesis — discernment, moral perception)" — love is to be guided and informed by knowledge and discernment, not blind or undiscriminating; a wise and intelligent love.

The puritan Henry notes the balance: love and knowledge go together — love without knowledge is misguided, knowledge without love is barren; Paul prays for both, that their love be discerning. The verse displays Paul's prayer that their love abound in knowledge.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul prays "that your love may abound (overflow, increase) yet more and more," their existing love growing greater (Barnes, Robertson); "in knowledge (full knowledge) and in all judgment (discernment)," love guided and informed by knowledge, a wise and intelligent love, not blind (Barnes, Calvin); love and knowledge going together (Henry). The verse displays love abounding in knowledge. The verse marks the prayer: love abound. The verse displays the increase: more and more. The verse displays the guidance: knowledge and judgment. The verse displays the balance: discerning love. The prayer displays love growing and guided by knowledge. The verse displays the love: abounding. The verse displays the knowledge: full. The verse displays the discernment: all judgment. Paul's prayer that their love abound in knowledge and discernment displays the first petition of his intercession: that the Philippians' love grow ever greater, yet guided by full knowledge and moral discernment — a wise and intelligent love, not undiscriminating. The verse displays the prayer for discerning love, that they approve the excellent (v.10).
10:
"That ye may approve things that are excellent; that ye may be sincere and without offence till the day of Christ;" The brief verse notes the prayer that they approve the excellent and be sincere till the day of Christ. The grammatical voices note the discernment. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of the abounding, knowing love: "that ye may approve (test and approve, or distinguish) things that are excellent (the things that differ, the better things)" — that they may discern and choose what is truly best.

On "that ye may be sincere and without offence," the witnesses note the character. Barnes and Calvin note the goal: "sincere (eilikrines — pure, unmixed, genuine, tested by sunlight) and without offence (blameless, not causing others to stumble)" — inwardly pure and outwardly blameless.

On "till the day of Christ," the grammatical-historical note the perseverance: this purity and blamelessness is to continue "till the day of Christ" — maintained steadfastly until his return; the goal is a holiness that endures to the end. The verse displays the prayer that they approve the excellent and be sincere.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of discerning love — "that ye may approve (test and choose) things that are excellent (the better things)," discerning what is best (Barnes, Robertson); the goal "sincere (pure, genuine) and without offence (blameless)," inwardly pure and outwardly blameless (Barnes, Calvin); "till the day of Christ," maintained steadfastly until his return (grammatical-historical). The verse displays approving the excellent. The verse marks the discernment: approve the excellent. The verse displays the purity: sincere. The verse displays the blamelessness: without offence. The verse displays the perseverance: till the day of Christ. The prayer displays believers discerning and pure to the end. The verse displays the approving: of the best. The verse displays the sincerity: genuine. The verse displays the day: of Christ. The prayer that they approve the excellent and be sincere till the day of Christ displays the aim of discerning love: that the Philippians may test and choose what is truly best, and so be pure and blameless — a sincerity and holiness that endures steadfastly until the day of Christ. The verse displays the prayer for sincere holiness, filled with righteousness (v.11).
11:
"Being filled with the fruits of righteousness, which are by Jesus Christ, unto the glory and praise of God." The brief verse notes being filled with the fruits of righteousness to God's glory. The grammatical voices note the fruitfulness. Barnes and Robertson note the further mark: "being filled with the fruits of righteousness" — the inward purity issues in a full harvest of righteous deeds; the holy life is fruitful in every good work.

On "which are by Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Calvin note that these fruits are "by (through) Jesus Christ" — produced not by human effort alone but through Christ, who is the source of all true righteousness in the believer; the fruit grows from union with him.

On "unto the glory and praise of God," the grammatical-historical note the end: the whole aim is "the glory and praise of God" — the fruits of righteousness, produced by Christ, redound to God's glory; the believer's holiness exists for God's praise. The verse displays being filled with the fruits of righteousness to God's glory.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "being filled with the fruits of righteousness," the inward purity issuing in a full harvest of righteous deeds (Barnes, Robertson); "by (through) Jesus Christ," produced through Christ the source of righteousness, the fruit growing from union with him (Barnes, Calvin); "unto the glory and praise of God," the whole aim being God's glory (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruits of righteousness. The verse marks the fruitfulness: filled with fruits. The verse displays the source: by Jesus Christ. The verse displays the end: God's glory. The verse displays the union: with Christ. The fruitfulness displays the holy life yielding fruit to God's glory. The verse displays the fruits: of righteousness. The verse displays the Christ: the source. The verse displays the glory: of God. Being filled with the fruits of righteousness to God's glory displays the consummation of Paul's prayer: that the Philippians' discerning love and sincere holiness may issue in a full harvest of righteous deeds — produced through Jesus Christ — all redounding to the glory and praise of God. The verse displays the fruitful holiness God's grace produces, closing the prayer and turning to Paul's bonds (vv.12-26).
12:
"But I would ye should understand, brethren, that the things which happened unto me have fallen out rather unto the furtherance of the gospel;" The witnesses note that Paul's imprisonment furthered the gospel. On "the things which happened unto me," the grammatical voices note the circumstances. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns to his own situation: "the things which happened unto me" — his arrest, imprisonment, and trials at Rome, which the Philippians might have feared had hindered his work.

On "have fallen out rather unto the furtherance of the gospel," the witnesses note the surprising result. Barnes and Calvin note the glad news: far from hindering, his sufferings "have fallen out (turned out) rather unto the furtherance (prokope — the advance, progress) of the gospel" — God had overruled his imprisonment to advance the very cause it seemed to threaten.

The puritan Henry notes the providence: God brings good out of evil; what seemed a setback to the gospel God turned into its furtherance, so that Paul's chains served Christ. The verse displays that Paul's imprisonment furthered the gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to "the things which happened unto me" — his imprisonment and trials at Rome, which might seem to have hindered his work (Barnes, Robertson); but they "have fallen out (turned out) rather unto the furtherance (advance) of the gospel," God overruling his imprisonment to advance the cause (Barnes, Calvin); God bringing good out of evil, his chains serving Christ (Henry). The verse displays the gospel furthered. The verse marks the circumstances: what happened to Paul. The verse displays the result: furtherance of the gospel. The verse displays the surprise: rather than hindrance. The verse displays the providence: good from evil. The result displays Paul's bonds advancing the gospel. The verse displays the imprisonment: Paul's. The verse displays the furtherance: of the gospel. The verse displays the overruling: of God. That Paul's imprisonment furthered the gospel displays the overruling providence of God: the very trials that seemed to threaten the apostle's work — his arrest and bonds at Rome — God turned to the advance of the gospel, so that what looked like a setback became its furtherance. The verse displays the gospel advanced by Paul's bonds, made manifest in the palace (v.13).
13:
"So that my bonds in Christ are manifest in all the palace, and in all other places;" The brief verse notes Paul's bonds made known throughout the palace. The grammatical voices note the witness. Barnes and Robertson note the first way his imprisonment furthered the gospel: "my bonds in Christ (my chains, borne for Christ) are manifest (become known, openly recognized) in all the palace (the praetorium — the imperial guard, or the praetorian barracks)" — his imprisonment became a testimony.

On "in all the palace," the witnesses note the reach. Barnes and Calvin note that even in the praetorium — among the imperial guard and the household of Caesar — it became known that Paul was a prisoner for Christ, not for any crime; the gospel reached the very heart of imperial power.

On "in all other places," the grammatical-historical note the spread: beyond the palace, "in all other places" the reason for his bonds became known; his testimony spread widely. The verse displays Paul's bonds made known throughout the palace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the first way his imprisonment furthered the gospel — "my bonds in Christ are manifest (become known) in all the palace (the praetorium, the imperial guard)," his chains a testimony (Barnes, Robertson); even among the guard and Caesar's household it became known he was a prisoner for Christ, not for crime, the gospel reaching the heart of imperial power (Barnes, Calvin); and "in all other places" his testimony spread (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his bonds made known. The verse marks the bonds: in Christ. The verse displays the place: all the palace. The verse displays the reach: imperial guard. The verse displays the spread: all other places. The witness displays Paul's chains testifying to Christ. The verse displays the bonds: manifest. The verse displays the palace: reached. The verse displays the testimony: spread. Paul's bonds made known throughout the palace display the first fruit of his imprisonment: it became openly known, even among the imperial guard and the household of Caesar, that he was a prisoner for Christ — so that his chains themselves bore witness to the gospel at the very heart of Rome. The verse displays the testimony of his bonds, emboldening the brethren (v.14).
14:
"And many of the brethren in the Lord, waxing confident by my bonds, are much more bold to speak the word without fear." The brief verse notes the brethren emboldened by Paul's bonds to preach fearlessly. The grammatical voices note the second fruit. Barnes and Robertson note the second way his imprisonment advanced the gospel: "many of the brethren in the Lord, waxing confident (growing bold, gaining courage) by my bonds, are much more bold to speak the word without fear" — Paul's steadfast example in chains emboldened other believers.

On "waxing confident by my bonds," the witnesses note the surprising effect. Barnes and Calvin note the paradox: Paul's imprisonment, which might have frightened others into silence, instead gave them courage — seeing his constancy and God's support, they preached the more boldly.

The grammatical-historical note the courage: the brethren now spoke "the word (the gospel) without fear" — Paul's chains, far from silencing the gospel, multiplied its preachers and emboldened them. The verse displays the brethren emboldened by Paul's bonds.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second way his imprisonment advanced the gospel — "many of the brethren, waxing confident (growing bold) by my bonds, are much more bold to speak the word without fear," Paul's steadfast example emboldening them (Barnes, Robertson); the paradox that his imprisonment gave courage rather than fear, seeing his constancy and God's support (Barnes, Calvin); they now preached the gospel "without fear" (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the brethren emboldened. The verse marks the courage: waxing confident. The verse displays the cause: by Paul's bonds. The verse displays the boldness: speak without fear. The verse displays the paradox: chains emboldening. The boldness displays the brethren preaching the more for Paul's bonds. The verse displays the brethren: emboldened. The verse displays the bonds: their courage. The verse displays the word: spoken freely. The brethren emboldened by Paul's bonds to preach fearlessly display the second fruit of his imprisonment: his steadfast example in chains, far from silencing others, gave many believers fresh courage, so that they spoke the gospel the more boldly and without fear. The verse displays the gospel multiplied through his bonds, preached from mixed motives (vv.15-17).
15:
"Some indeed preach Christ even of envy and strife; and some also of good will:" The brief verse notes that some preach Christ from envy, others from good will. The grammatical voices note the two motives. Barnes and Robertson note Paul distinguishes two groups now preaching: "some indeed preach Christ even of envy and strife (from jealousy and rivalry, ill-disposed toward Paul); and some also of good will (from sincere affection and right motives)."

On "envy and strife," the witnesses note the bad motive. Barnes and Calvin note that some preached Christ from envy of Paul — perhaps jealous of his influence, or seeking to build their own party — using even the preaching of the true gospel as a means of rivalry against him.

The grammatical-historical note the distinction: the difference was not in the message (both preached Christ) but in the motive — envy in the one, good will in the other; the same gospel preached from opposite hearts. The verse displays that some preach Christ from envy, others from good will.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul distinguishes two groups — "some preach Christ of envy and strife (from jealousy and rivalry); and some of good will (sincere affection)" (Barnes, Robertson); some preaching from envy of Paul, using even the true gospel as rivalry against him (Barnes, Calvin); the difference being in motive not message, the same gospel from opposite hearts (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the two motives. The verse marks the bad: envy and strife. The verse displays the good: good will. The verse displays the message: Christ, by both. The verse displays the difference: of motive. The distinction displays the same gospel preached from opposite hearts. The verse displays the envy: of some. The verse displays the good will: of others. The verse displays the Christ: preached by both. That some preach Christ from envy, others from good will, displays the mixed motives among the preachers stirred by Paul's bonds: some preached the true gospel from jealousy and rivalry against him, others from sincere affection — the same Christ proclaimed from opposite hearts. The verse displays the two motives, detailed in their aims (vv.16-17).
16:
"The one preach Christ of contention, not sincerely, supposing to add affliction to my bonds:" The brief verse notes the insincere preachers seeking to add to Paul's affliction. The grammatical voices note their motive. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the ill-disposed group: "the one preach Christ of contention (from party-spirit, faction, rivalry), not sincerely (not with pure motives)" — their preaching was a means of self-promotion and opposition.

On "supposing to add affliction to my bonds," the witnesses note their malice. Barnes and Calvin note their aim: "supposing to add affliction (trouble, distress) to my bonds" — they thought, by their successful preaching, to increase Paul's grief, perhaps by stirring envy or by undermining his influence while he lay helpless in prison.

The grammatical-historical note the irony: their malice failed of its purpose, for Paul, far from being grieved, rejoiced that Christ was preached (v.18); their ill will could not diminish his joy. The verse displays the insincere preachers seeking to add to Paul's affliction.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ill-disposed group — "the one preach Christ of contention (party-spirit, rivalry), not sincerely," their preaching self-promotion and opposition (Barnes, Robertson); "supposing to add affliction to my bonds," thinking to increase Paul's grief while he lay helpless (Barnes, Calvin); their malice failing, for Paul rejoiced instead (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the insincere preachers. The verse marks the motive: contention. The verse displays the insincerity: not sincerely. The verse displays the aim: add affliction. The verse displays the irony: malice failed. The preachers display ill will seeking to grieve Paul. The verse displays the contention: their spirit. The verse displays the affliction: intended. The verse displays the bonds: Paul's. The insincere preachers seeking to add to Paul's affliction display the malice of the ill-disposed group: they preached Christ from party-spirit and rivalry, not sincerely, thinking by their success to deepen the grief of the imprisoned apostle — a malice that would yet fail of its aim. The verse displays the bad motive, set against the sincere love of the others (v.17).
17:
"But the other of love, knowing that I am set for the defence of the gospel." The brief verse notes the sincere preachers who preach from love. The grammatical voices note the good motive. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the well-disposed group: "the other (preach Christ) of love" — from sincere love, both to Christ and to Paul; their preaching flowed from right affection.

On "knowing that I am set for the defence of the gospel," the witnesses note their understanding. Barnes and Calvin note their right perception: they preached "knowing (perceiving) that I am set (appointed, placed) for the defence (apologia — the vindication) of the gospel" — they understood that Paul's imprisonment was for the gospel's defence, and their preaching supported, not undermined, his cause.

The grammatical-historical note the contrast: over against the contentious preachers stand these — preaching from love, in sympathy with the imprisoned apostle and his work; their motive pure. The verse displays the sincere preachers who preach from love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the well-disposed group — "the other (preach Christ) of love," from sincere love to Christ and Paul (Barnes, Robertson); "knowing that I am set (appointed) for the defence of the gospel," understanding Paul's imprisonment was for the gospel and supporting his cause (Barnes, Calvin); over against the contentious, these preaching from love in sympathy with him (grammatical-historical). The verse displays preaching from love. The verse marks the motive: of love. The verse displays the understanding: set for the gospel's defence. The verse displays the sympathy: with Paul. The verse displays the contrast: with the contentious. The preachers display sincere love supporting Paul's cause. The verse displays the love: their motive. The verse displays the defence: of the gospel. The verse displays the support: of Paul. The sincere preachers who preach from love display the good motive over against the contentious: knowing that Paul was appointed for the defence of the gospel, they preached Christ from love — in sympathy with the imprisoned apostle, supporting and not undermining his cause. The verse displays the right motive, transcended by Paul's joy that Christ is preached (v.18).
18:
"What then? notwithstanding, every way, whether in pretence, or in truth, Christ is preached; and I therein do rejoice, yea, and will rejoice." The witnesses note Paul's joy that Christ is preached, whatever the motive. On "what then?", the grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul brushes aside the question of motives with a magnanimous "what then?" — what does it ultimately matter? — rising above any personal grievance.

On "whether in pretence, or in truth, Christ is preached," the witnesses note the one thing that matters. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul's largeness of heart: "whether in pretence (with false, self-serving motives) or in truth (sincerely), Christ is preached" — the great fact, that Christ is proclaimed, outweighs every consideration of the preachers' motives.

On "I therein do rejoice, yea, and will rejoice," the Reformed Calvin notes the selflessness: Paul rejoices, and resolves to keep rejoicing, simply because Christ is made known — forgetting his own injury in his joy for the gospel's spread; his concern is Christ's glory, not his own vindication. The verse displays Paul's joy that Christ is preached.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul brushes aside the question of motives — "what then?" — rising above personal grievance (Barnes, Robertson); "whether in pretence (false motives) or in truth (sincerely), Christ is preached," the great fact outweighing the preachers' motives (Barnes, Chrysostom); "I therein do rejoice, yea, and will rejoice," rejoicing and resolving to rejoice that Christ is known, forgetting his own injury (Calvin). The verse displays Paul's joy. The verse marks the question: what then? The verse displays the indifference: pretence or truth. The verse displays the fact: Christ is preached. The verse displays the joy: present and resolved. The joy displays Paul glad simply that Christ is proclaimed. The verse displays the preaching: of Christ. The verse displays the motive: set aside. The verse displays the rejoicing: of Paul. Paul's joy that Christ is preached, whatever the motive, displays the magnanimity of his heart: rising above all the malice and rivalry, he counts the one great fact — that Christ is proclaimed — as outweighing every consideration of motive, and so rejoices, and resolves to keep rejoicing, in the gospel's spread. The verse displays the apostle's selfless joy, grounded in hope of his deliverance (v.19).
19:
"For I know that this shall turn to my salvation through your prayer, and the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ," The brief verse notes Paul's confidence that all will turn to his salvation. The grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's settled confidence: "I know that this (his whole situation — imprisonment, the preaching, all of it) shall turn to my salvation" — either his deliverance from prison, or (more deeply) his final salvation; all things working for his good.

On "through your prayer," the witnesses note the means. Barnes and Calvin note that this good outcome comes "through your prayer" — Paul ascribes much to the intercession of the Philippians, valuing and relying on their prayers for him.

On "the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the deeper means: also "the supply (the abundant provision, the support) of the Spirit of Jesus Christ" — the Holy Spirit's supply of grace and strength, by which Paul is upheld; the Spirit is the Spirit of Jesus Christ (a witness to the bond of Son and Spirit). The verse displays Paul's confidence that all will turn to his salvation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's settled confidence — "I know that this shall turn to my salvation," his whole situation working for his good, whether deliverance or final salvation (Barnes, Robertson); "through your prayer," the intercession of the Philippians, which he values (Barnes, Calvin); "the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ," the Spirit's provision of grace upholding him (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays his confidence. The verse marks the assurance: turn to my salvation. The verse displays the means: your prayer. The verse displays the supply: the Spirit of Christ. The verse displays the bond: Son and Spirit. The confidence displays Paul upheld by prayer and the Spirit. The verse displays the salvation: assured. The verse displays the prayer: theirs. The verse displays the Spirit: supplying. Paul's confidence that all will turn to his salvation displays his serene assurance amid imprisonment: he knows his whole situation will work for his good — whether deliverance or final salvation — through the prayers of the Philippians and the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ. The verse displays the apostle's confidence, expressed in his earnest expectation (v.20).
20:
"According to my earnest expectation and my hope, that in nothing I shall be ashamed, but that with all boldness, as always, so now also Christ shall be magnified in my body, whether it be by life, or by death." The witnesses note Paul's hope that Christ be magnified in him, by life or death. On "my earnest expectation and my hope, that in nothing I shall be ashamed," the grammatical voices note the confidence. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's "earnest expectation (apokaradokia — eager, intense longing, the head stretched forward) and hope" that "in nothing I shall be ashamed" — that he will not be put to shame, his faith and courage not failing under trial.

On "with all boldness... Christ shall be magnified in my body," the witnesses note the aim. Barnes and Calvin note his great desire: "that with all boldness (freedom, courage)... Christ shall be magnified (exalted, made great) in my body" — that Christ may be honoured and glorified in and through Paul, in his very person.

On "whether it be by life, or by death," the Reformed Calvin notes the indifference: Paul cares not whether Christ is magnified "by life" (if he is released and lives on) "or by death" (if he is martyred) — he desires only that Christ be glorified, by whichever outcome. The verse displays Paul's hope that Christ be magnified in him, by life or death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's "earnest expectation (eager longing) and hope" that "in nothing I shall be ashamed," his faith and courage not failing (Barnes, Robertson); his desire "that with all boldness Christ shall be magnified (exalted) in my body," Christ honoured in his very person (Barnes, Calvin); "whether by life or by death," indifferent to his own fate so Christ be glorified (Calvin). The verse displays Christ magnified in Paul. The verse marks the expectation: earnest hope. The verse displays the confidence: not ashamed. The verse displays the aim: Christ magnified. The verse displays the indifference: life or death. The hope displays Paul desiring Christ glorified by any outcome. The verse displays the boldness: with all. The verse displays the body: Christ magnified. The verse displays the life or death: alike. Paul's hope that Christ be magnified in him, by life or death, displays the consuming aim of his heart: his one earnest longing is that he not be put to shame but that Christ be exalted in his very person — and whether this comes by his release and life or by his martyrdom matters nothing, so Christ be glorified. The verse displays the apostle's single aim, summed in his great confession (v.21).
21:
"For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain." The witnesses note Paul's great confession: living is Christ, dying is gain. On "for to me to live is Christ," the grammatical voices note the meaning of life. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's profound summary: "to me to live is Christ" — for Paul, life itself is summed up in Christ; Christ is the aim, the substance, the whole meaning of his living — to live is to serve, know, and glorify Christ.

On "and to die is gain," the witnesses note the meaning of death. Barnes and Calvin note the astonishing reckoning: "to die is gain" — death, far from loss, is positive gain for the believer, for it brings him to be with Christ (v.23), freed from sin and sorrow into perfect fellowship with his Lord.

The puritan Henry notes the summary: here is the whole of the Christian's life and hope — Christ is his life now, and death will be his gain; he cannot lose, for life is Christ and death is more of Christ. The verse displays Paul's great confession: living is Christ, dying is gain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "to me to live is Christ" — life itself summed up in Christ, the aim and substance of Paul's living, to serve and glorify him (Barnes, Robertson); "to die is gain" — death positive gain, bringing the believer to be with Christ, freed from sin into perfect fellowship (Barnes, Calvin); the whole of the Christian's life and hope, Christ his life and death his gain (Henry). The verse displays the great confession. The verse marks the life: is Christ. The verse displays the death: is gain. The verse displays the meaning: Christ all. The verse displays the hope: more of Christ. The confession displays Paul's life and death both bound up in Christ. The verse displays the living: Christ. The verse displays the dying: gain. The verse displays the Christ: all in all. Paul's great confession — living is Christ, dying is gain — displays the heart of the believer's existence: for Paul, life itself is wholly summed up in Christ, its aim and substance; and death is not loss but gain, for it brings him into perfect fellowship with the Lord he loves. The verse displays the believer's life and hope in Christ, weighed against the desire to depart (vv.22-24).
22:
"But if I live in the flesh, this is the fruit of my labour: yet what I shall choose I wot not." The brief verse notes Paul's uncertainty whether to prefer life or death. The grammatical voices note the value of continued life. Barnes and Robertson note Paul weighs the alternative: "if I live in the flesh (continue in this earthly life), this is the fruit of my labour" — continued life means more fruitful labour, more souls won and saints built up; living has its value.

On "what I shall choose I wot not," the witnesses note the dilemma. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul's genuine perplexity: "what I shall choose I wot (know) not" — were the choice his, he cannot tell which to prefer; both alternatives, life and death, are good, and he is torn between them.

The Reformed Calvin notes the tension: Paul is caught between his own desire (to depart and be with Christ) and his usefulness to the church (to live and labour); the very difficulty of the choice shows the goodness of both. The verse displays Paul's uncertainty whether to prefer life or death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul weighs the alternative — "if I live in the flesh, this is the fruit of my labour," continued life meaning more fruitful labour and souls won (Barnes, Robertson); "what I shall choose I wot (know) not," genuinely perplexed, unable to tell which to prefer, both being good (Barnes, Chrysostom); caught between his desire and his usefulness, the difficulty showing both good (Calvin). The verse displays Paul's uncertainty. The verse marks the alternative: living the fruit of labour. The verse displays the dilemma: I know not. The verse displays the value: of continued life. The verse displays the tension: desire vs usefulness. The uncertainty displays Paul torn between two goods. The verse displays the life: fruitful. The verse displays the choice: unknown. The verse displays the perplexity: genuine. Paul's uncertainty whether to prefer life or death displays the tension in his heart: continued life would mean more fruitful labour for Christ and the church, yet he cannot tell which to choose — for both living and dying are good, and he is genuinely torn between them. The verse displays the apostle's dilemma, sharpened by his desire to be with Christ (v.23).
23:
"For I am in a strait betwixt two, having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is far better:" The witnesses note Paul's desire to depart and be with Christ. On "I am in a strait betwixt two," the grammatical voices note the pressure. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: Paul is "in a strait betwixt two (synechomai — hemmed in, pressed, constrained from both sides)" — pulled in two directions by the two good alternatives, life and death.

On "having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ," the witnesses note his personal longing. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's own preference: "having a desire to depart (analysai — to loose, as a ship from its moorings, or strike a tent) and to be with Christ" — for himself, he longs to die, for death means departing this life to be immediately with Christ.

On "which is far better," the witnesses note the intermediate state. Barnes, Calvin, and the grammatical-historical note the strong assurance: to be with Christ is "far better (much more, better by far)" — and this immediately upon death; the verse is a clear witness that the souls of believers are consciously present with Christ at death (against any notion of soul-sleep), in a state far better than this life. The verse displays Paul's desire to depart and be with Christ.

The witnesses agree: Paul is "in a strait betwixt two (hemmed in, pressed from both sides)," pulled by the two good alternatives (Barnes, Robertson); his own longing is "to depart (to loose, as a ship from moorings) and to be with Christ," for death means departing to be immediately with Christ (Barnes, Calvin); and to be with Christ is "far better," and this at once upon death — a clear witness that believers' souls are consciously with Christ at death, far better than this life (grammatical-historical, Calvin). The verse displays the desire to be with Christ. The verse marks the strait: pressed between two. The verse displays the desire: to depart. The verse displays the goal: with Christ. The verse displays the assurance: far better, at once. The desire displays Paul longing to be with Christ at death. The verse displays the departing: longed for. The verse displays the Christ: with him. The verse displays the better: by far. Paul's desire to depart and be with Christ displays his personal longing and a witness to the believer's hope at death: hemmed in between life and death, he longs for himself to depart and be at once with Christ — a state far better than this life, in which the souls of believers are consciously present with their Lord. The verse displays the blessedness of departing to Christ, yet weighed against the church's need (v.24).
24:
"Nevertheless to abide in the flesh is more needful for you." The brief verse notes that Paul's remaining is more needful for the church. The grammatical voices note the turn. Barnes and Robertson note that, against his own desire to depart, Paul recognizes another claim: "to abide (remain) in the flesh (in this earthly life) is more needful for you (the Philippians and the churches)" — his continued life would serve their good.

On "more needful for you," the witnesses note the selflessness. Barnes and Clarke note Paul's love: though to depart would be his own gain, to remain would benefit the church; and he is willing to forgo his own preference for their sake — their need outweighs his desire.

The grammatical-historical note the resolution: this consideration tips the balance — Paul concludes (v.25) that he will remain, not because he prefers it for himself, but because the church needs him. The verse displays that Paul's remaining is more needful for the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: against his own desire, Paul recognizes "to abide (remain) in the flesh is more needful for you," his continued life serving the church's good (Barnes, Robertson); his love willing to forgo his own gain for their sake, their need outweighing his desire (Barnes, Clarke); this tipping the balance toward remaining (grammatical-historical). The verse displays remaining for their sake. The verse marks the turn: nevertheless. The verse displays the need: more needful for you. The verse displays the selflessness: forgoing his gain. The verse displays the resolution: he will remain. The turn displays Paul choosing the church's good over his own desire. The verse displays the abiding: in the flesh. The verse displays the need: theirs. The verse displays the love: selfless. That Paul's remaining is more needful for the church displays the selflessness of his love: though to depart would be his own gain, he recognizes that to remain in this life would better serve the Philippians and the churches — and their need outweighs his own longing. The verse displays the apostle's self-sacrificing choice, issuing in confident hope (vv.25-26).
25:
"And having this confidence, I know that I shall abide and continue with you all for your furtherance and joy of faith;" The brief verse notes Paul's confidence that he will remain for their furtherance and joy. The grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's persuasion: "having this confidence, I know that I shall abide (remain alive) and continue with you all" — convinced that the church's need will be met, he expects to be released and to remain for their sake.

On "for your furtherance and joy of faith," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the end of his remaining: "for your furtherance (advancement, progress) and joy of faith" — that the Philippians may grow in faith and rejoice in it; his continued ministry would serve their spiritual progress and gladness.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the confidence: Paul's assurance that he will be spared rests not on certainty of the outcome but on his sense of the church's need and God's purpose to serve it through him. The verse displays Paul's confidence that he will remain for their furtherance and joy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's persuasion — "having this confidence, I know that I shall abide (remain) and continue with you all," expecting release for the church's sake (Barnes, Robertson); the purpose "for your furtherance (progress) and joy of faith," that they grow in faith and rejoice (Barnes, Calvin); the confidence resting on the church's need and God's purpose (Wesley). The verse displays his confidence to remain. The verse marks the assurance: I shall abide. The verse displays the continuance: with you all. The verse displays the purpose: furtherance and joy. The verse displays the ground: the church's need. The confidence displays Paul expecting to remain for their good. The verse displays the abiding: expected. The verse displays the furtherance: of faith. The verse displays the joy: of faith. Paul's confidence that he will remain for their furtherance and joy displays his expectation grounded in love: persuaded that the church needs him, he is confident he will be spared and continue with them — for the advancement of their faith and the increase of their joy. The verse displays the apostle's hope of return, that their rejoicing may abound (v.26).
26:
"That your rejoicing may be more abundant in Jesus Christ for me by my coming to you again." The brief verse notes that Paul's return would increase their rejoicing in Christ. The grammatical voices note the aim. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of his expected release: "that your rejoicing (boasting, glorying) may be more abundant (overflow) in Jesus Christ for me by my coming to you again" — his return would give the Philippians fresh cause for rejoicing.

On "in Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the object. Barnes and Calvin note that the rejoicing is "in Jesus Christ" — their gladness at Paul's return would be a rejoicing in Christ, for it is Christ who would have spared and restored him; the joy is directed to the Lord, not merely to Paul.

The grammatical-historical note the affection: Paul's coming again would so gladden the Philippians that their joy in Christ would overflow; such was the love between them. The verse displays that Paul's return would increase their rejoicing in Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of his expected release — "that your rejoicing (glorying) may be more abundant (overflow) in Jesus Christ for me by my coming again," his return giving fresh cause for rejoicing (Barnes, Robertson); the rejoicing "in Jesus Christ," directed to the Lord who would restore him, not merely to Paul (Barnes, Calvin); his coming so gladdening them that their joy in Christ would overflow (grammatical-historical). The verse displays increased rejoicing. The verse marks the aim: rejoicing more abundant. The verse displays the object: in Jesus Christ. The verse displays the occasion: Paul's return. The verse displays the affection: overflowing joy. The aim displays the church's joy in Christ increased by Paul's return. The verse displays the rejoicing: abundant. The verse displays the Christ: its object. The verse displays the coming: again. That Paul's return would increase their rejoicing in Christ displays the affection between apostle and church: his coming again to them would give the Philippians fresh and overflowing cause for rejoicing — a joy directed to Jesus Christ, who would have spared and restored him. The verse displays the joy of his hoped-for return, prompting a charge to worthy living (v.27).
27:
"Only let your conversation be as it becometh the gospel of Christ: that whether I come and see you, or else be absent, I may hear of your affairs, that ye stand fast in one spirit, with one mind striving together for the faith of the gospel;" The witnesses note the call to a worthy walk, standing fast and striving together for the gospel. On "let your conversation be as it becometh the gospel of Christ," the grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's central exhortation: "let your conversation (politeuesthe — live as citizens, conduct your public life) be as it becometh (worthily of) the gospel of Christ" — their whole manner of life is to match the gospel they profess.

On "whether I come... or be absent, I may hear... that ye stand fast in one spirit," the witnesses note the steadfast unity. Barnes and Henry note Paul's wish: that whether present or absent, he may hear they "stand fast (stand firm) in one spirit, with one mind (one soul)" — firm and united, not divided by faction.

On "striving together for the faith of the gospel," the Reformed Calvin notes the common contest: "striving together (synathlountes — contending side by side, as athletes or soldiers) for the faith of the gospel" — united in the common struggle to defend and advance the gospel against all opposition. The verse displays the call to a worthy walk, standing fast and striving together.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's central charge — "let your conversation (live as citizens) be worthily of the gospel of Christ," the whole life matching the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); his wish that, present or absent, he hear they "stand fast in one spirit, with one mind," firm and united, not factious (Barnes, Henry); "striving together (contending side by side) for the faith of the gospel," united in the common struggle (Calvin). The verse displays the worthy walk. The verse marks the charge: worthy of the gospel. The verse displays the steadfastness: stand fast. The verse displays the unity: one spirit, one mind. The verse displays the contest: striving together. The call displays believers living worthily, firm and united. The verse displays the conversation: worthy. The verse displays the standing: fast. The verse displays the striving: together. The call to a worthy walk, standing fast and striving together for the gospel, displays the heart of Paul's exhortation: the Philippians are to live as citizens worthy of the gospel — standing firm in one spirit and one mind, contending side by side for the faith — that whether present or absent, Paul may hear of their steadfast unity. The verse displays the worthy, united walk, undaunted by adversaries (v.28).
28:
"And in nothing terrified by your adversaries: which is to them an evident token of perdition, but to you of salvation, and that of God." The brief verse notes the call to fearlessness, a token of perdition to foes and salvation to the saints. The grammatical voices note the courage. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge: "in nothing terrified (frightened, alarmed, as startled horses) by your adversaries (opponents, persecutors)" — the believers are to stand undaunted, fearless before those who oppose them.

On "an evident token of perdition... but to you of salvation," the witnesses note the double sign. Barnes and Calvin note that their fearless steadfastness is "an evident token (a clear sign, proof)" — "to them (the adversaries) of perdition (their coming destruction)" and "to you of salvation" — the believers' courage signifies the foes' ruin and the saints' deliverance.

On "and that of God," the grammatical-historical note the source: this salvation, and the very courage that signifies it, are "of God" — from him, his gift and work; the believers' fearlessness is itself a token that God is with them. The verse displays the call to fearlessness, a token of perdition and salvation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge — "in nothing terrified (frightened) by your adversaries," standing undaunted before opponents (Barnes, Robertson); their fearless steadfastness "an evident token (clear sign)," "to them of perdition (their destruction)" and "to you of salvation," signifying the foes' ruin and the saints' deliverance (Barnes, Calvin); "and that of God," the salvation and the courage being God's gift (grammatical-historical). The verse displays fearlessness as a token. The verse marks the courage: in nothing terrified. The verse displays the adversaries: opposed. The verse displays the sign: perdition and salvation. The verse displays the source: of God. The fearlessness displays the saints' courage a token of God's saving them. The verse displays the terror: refused. The verse displays the token: double. The verse displays the God: its source. The call to fearlessness, a token of perdition to foes and salvation to the saints, displays the meaning of Christian courage under persecution: the believers' undaunted steadfastness before their adversaries is a clear sign — of the opponents' coming ruin and the saints' salvation — and this, with the very courage that shows it, is the gift of God. The verse displays the token of fearless faith, grounded in the gift of suffering (v.29).
29:
"For unto you it is given in the behalf of Christ, not only to believe on him, but also to suffer for his sake;" The witnesses note that to believe and to suffer for Christ are both granted as a gift — with the question of faith as a gift. On "unto you it is given in the behalf of Christ," the grammatical voices note the gracious grant. Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, with Clarke, note that "it is given (echaristhe — granted as a gift, a favour, a privilege)" — both believing and suffering for Christ are graciously bestowed; it is a privilege, an honour, to be allowed to believe on Christ and to suffer for him.

On "not only to believe on him, but also to suffer for his sake," interpreters address the gift. The Reformed Calvin presses that faith itself, no less than constancy in suffering, is an unmerited gift of God — the knowledge of God being too high for our own attaining, so that faith is bestowed for Christ's sake, excluding all merit (against the schoolmen who made later grace a reward of merit). The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley emphasize rather the granting of the privilege — that it is given as a favour and honour both to believe and to suffer; Wesley calls it "a special token of God's love."

The witnesses note the privilege of suffering: it is represented as an honour, not merely a hardship, to suffer for Christ. The verse displays that to believe and to suffer for Christ are both granted as a gift.

The witnesses agree the substance: "it is given (granted as a gift, a favour)" both "to believe on him" and "to suffer for his sake" — both being privileges graciously bestowed, suffering for Christ an honour, not merely a hardship (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke, Wesley). But the weight divides them: Calvin presses that faith itself is an unmerited gift of God, no less than constancy in suffering, bestowed for Christ's sake and excluding all merit (against the schoolmen); Barnes, Ellicott, and Wesley emphasize the granting of the privilege — the honour of being allowed to believe and to suffer, a special token of God's love. The verse displays the gift of faith and suffering. The verse marks the grant: it is given. The verse displays the debate: faith a gift, or the privilege granted. The verse displays the believing: granted. The verse displays the suffering: an honour. The grant displays both faith and suffering as gifts of grace. The verse displays the gift: of God. The verse displays the faith: bestowed. The verse displays the suffering: a privilege. That to believe and to suffer for Christ are both granted as a gift displays the gracious privilege of the Christian life and a doctrinal nerve: it is given — as a favour and honour — both to believe on Christ and to suffer for him, with Calvin pressing that faith itself is an unmerited gift of God, and the others the granting of the privilege of believing and suffering. The verse displays the gift of faith and the honour of suffering, in the common conflict (v.30).
30:
"Having the same conflict which ye saw in me, and now hear to be in me." The brief verse notes the Philippians sharing the same conflict as Paul. The grammatical voices note the shared struggle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's conclusion: the Philippians have "the same conflict (agon — contest, struggle, as in the arena) which ye saw in me, and now hear to be in me" — they share the same struggle of suffering for Christ that they witnessed in Paul.

On "which ye saw in me," the witnesses note the past witness. Barnes and Calvin note that the Philippians had "seen" Paul's conflict — when he was beaten and imprisoned at Philippi itself (Acts 16) — and "now hear" of his present conflict in his Roman imprisonment; their own sufferings mirror his.

The grammatical-historical note the fellowship: the believers are not alone in their trials but share the same contest as the apostle and all the saints; fellow-sufferers in the same cause, they are bound together. The verse displays the Philippians sharing the same conflict as Paul.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Philippians have "the same conflict (contest, struggle) which ye saw in me, and now hear to be in me," sharing the same struggle of suffering for Christ (Barnes, Robertson); they had "seen" Paul's conflict at Philippi (Acts 16) and "now hear" of his Roman imprisonment, their sufferings mirroring his (Barnes, Calvin); fellow-sufferers in the same cause, bound together (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the shared conflict. The verse marks the conflict: the same as Paul's. The verse displays the witness: ye saw in me. The verse displays the present: now hear. The verse displays the fellowship: fellow-sufferers. The conflict displays the Philippians sharing Paul's struggle for Christ. The verse displays the conflict: shared. The verse displays the seeing: of Paul's. The verse displays the hearing: of his now. The Philippians sharing the same conflict as Paul displays the fellowship of Christian suffering: their own struggle for Christ is the same contest they had seen in Paul at Philippi and now hear of in his imprisonment — fellow-sufferers with the apostle and all the saints in the one cause. The verse displays the shared conflict of the faithful, closing the chapter on suffering and steadfastness for the gospel.
